cards.title: What "AI System" Means for Testers
cards.teaser: Before you can test an AI system, you need a clear definition of what you're actually testing — and it is probably not what you think.

runs.text: An AI system, for testing purposes, is any system whose behavior is substantially determined by patterns learned from data rather than by logic explicitly written by a programmer. That distinction is the load-bearing idea of this entire book.

Classical software encodes rules: if input X, do Y. You can trace a bug from a failing test back to the line of code that wrote the wrong rule. An AI system encodes statistical associations: given inputs shaped like X, produce outputs shaped like Y, where "shaped like" is defined by millions of training examples, not by a developer's if-statement. The program is, in a real sense, the data.
This book follows the ISTQB® CT-AI v1.0 syllabus structure to support exam prep, but it is not an official ISTQB® study guide.
This book is about AI as the system under test — the inverse of using AI to do testing work.

That second distinction matters because there is a growing body of work on using AI tools to generate test cases, write test scripts, or prioritize test runs. That is a different discipline. Here, the AI model is the subject of your scrutiny: you are trying to determine whether it works correctly, reliably, fairly, and safely.
Throughout this book, "AI system" refers to ML-based systems unless otherwise noted. Pure rule-based expert systems are occasionally referenced for contrast, but they are not the primary subject.

Testing an AI system is less like proofreading a recipe and more like auditing a chef — you cannot just check the instructions because the behavior emerged from years of experience that was never written down.

cards.title: Narrow AI vs General AI
cards.teaser: Every AI system in production today is narrow — it does one thing, and it fails when you push it outside the distribution it was trained on.

runs.text: Narrow AI, also called weak AI or applied AI, is a system trained to perform a specific task or a tightly bounded set of tasks. Your spam filter is narrow AI. Your fraud detection model is narrow AI. A large language model that summarizes support tickets is narrow AI, even though it feels broad — it was trained on language data and it fails predictably when you ask it to do things that require grounded physical reasoning or live knowledge.

Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is the hypothetical ability of a machine to perform any intellectual task a human can perform, including tasks it has never encountered before. No deployed system today meets that bar. AGI remains a research direction, not a product.

Why does this matter for testing? Because narrow AI has a defined operating envelope — the distribution of inputs it was trained on — and behavior outside that envelope is undefined. Your test strategy must include:

items.text: Characterizing the intended operating envelope (what inputs is this model designed to handle?)
Designing tests that probe the boundary of that envelope
Documenting the expected behavior when inputs fall outside it

runs.text: The most common mistake is treating a narrow AI as if it generalizes. It does not. It interpolates within its training distribution and extrapolates poorly beyond it.

A model that scores 97% accuracy on your test set is not a model that works on 97% of real-world inputs — unless your test set faithfully represents the real-world distribution, which it usually does not.
A narrow AI is like a very talented specialist surgeon: extraordinary within their domain, and genuinely dangerous if asked to perform a procedure outside their training.

cards.title: Supervised Learning
cards.teaser: Supervised learning is the most common ML paradigm — and understanding its structure tells you exactly why labeled data quality is the dominant risk factor in the systems you test.

runs.text: In supervised learning, a model is trained on a dataset where each input example is paired with a correct output label. The training process adjusts the model's internal parameters until it can map inputs to outputs with acceptable error on examples it has seen. The model is then evaluated on a held-out test set — examples it was not trained on — to estimate how well it generalizes.
The three-part vocabulary you need:

items.text: Training set
— the labeled examples the model learns from. Larger is generally better, but quality matters more than quantity.
Validation set
— held out during training and used to tune hyperparameters and catch overfitting (performing well on training data but poorly on new data).
Test set
— touched only once, at the end, to produce an unbiased estimate of model performance. Using the test set repeatedly to make modeling decisions is a form of data leakage.

runs.text: From a tester's perspective, supervised learning creates a specific risk structure:

items.text: If the labels in the training data are wrong, the model learns to be wrong in the same systematic way.
If the training distribution does not match the deployment distribution, reported accuracy on the test set is an overestimate of real-world performance.
The model has no mechanism to know when it is wrong — it produces an output regardless.

runs.text: Ask for the data labeling process before you design your test approach. Who labeled the training data, with what guidelines, and what inter-annotator agreement was measured? Systematic labeling errors are the hardest bugs to find after the fact.

Training a supervised model is like grading a student with a fixed answer key — the student gets very good at matching the answer key, and you only discover how brittle that is when you ask a question the key does not cover.

cards.title: Unsupervised Learning
cards.teaser: Unsupervised learning has no answer key — the model finds structure in unlabeled data, which means you cannot evaluate it by counting correct predictions.

runs.text: In unsupervised learning, the training data has no labels. The model's job is to find latent structure — patterns, groupings, or compressed representations — that exist in the data without being told what to look for.
The two most common unsupervised techniques:

items.text: Clustering

— grouping inputs so that members of the same cluster are more similar to each other than to members of other clusters. K-means, DBSCAN, and hierarchical clustering are common algorithms. The output is a segment assignment, not a prediction of a known category.
Dimensionality reduction

— projecting high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation that preserves important structure. PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP are common algorithms. Often used as a preprocessing step before supervised learning, or for visualization.

runs.text: Testing unsupervised systems requires a different mindset. You cannot measure accuracy because there is no ground truth to compare against. Instead, you evaluate:

items.text: Cluster cohesion
— are items within a cluster actually similar by domain criteria?
Cluster separation
— are clusters meaningfully distinct?
Stability
— do you get the same clusters if you run the algorithm twice, or on a slightly different sample?
Downstream validity
— if these clusters are used to drive a business decision, does that decision produce better outcomes than a baseline?

runs.text: The failure mode for unsupervised systems is often invisible: the model groups things in ways that are mathematically coherent but semantically meaningless or actively harmful (encoding protected attributes, for instance).
Unsupervised models can encode and amplify biases present in the input data without producing any metric that reveals this. Fairness evaluation requires deliberate, domain-specific analysis.

Unsupervised learning is like asking someone to sort a pile of foreign coins they have never seen before — they will find a grouping that feels consistent, but you cannot know if it matches the grouping an expert would choose until a domain expert reviews the result.

cards.title: Reinforcement Learning
cards.teaser: Reinforcement learning trains a policy by rewarding good behavior — but whatever you measure becomes what the system optimizes, and that proxy metric is rarely a perfect stand-in for what you actually want.

runs.text: In reinforcement learning (RL), an agent interacts with an environment. At each step, the agent takes an action, the environment transitions to a new state, and the agent receives a reward signal. Over many episodes, the agent learns a policy — a mapping from states to actions — that maximizes cumulative reward.
The three structural concepts:

items.text: Agent
— the model that makes decisions (the thing you are training and testing).
Environment
— the world the agent acts in, which can be a simulation, a game engine, a real physical system, or an API.
Reward function
— the signal that defines what "good" behavior means. This is the most important design decision in RL, and it is almost always specified by a human.

runs.text: The policy update loop: the agent tries actions, observes rewards, updates its internal model of which actions lead to good outcomes (the value function), and uses that updated model to choose better actions in future episodes.
For testers, reinforcement learning introduces risks that supervised learning does not:

items.text: Reward hacking
— the agent finds a way to maximize the reward metric that does not correspond to the intended behavior. If your reward is clicks, the agent may learn to be alarming rather than informative.
Environment coupling
— the agent is trained in one environment and deployed in a different one. Distribution shift is often more severe in RL than in supervised settings.
Non-stationarity
— in some RL deployments, the agent's actions change the environment over time, which changes the distribution of future states, which changes the effective training signal.

runs.text: Defining the reward function is a requirements problem, not a modeling problem. If you are reviewing an RL system and cannot find a written specification of what the reward function was intended to incentivize, treat that as a critical gap in the design.

Reinforcement learning is like training a dog with treats — the dog quickly learns exactly what behavior earns a treat, which is only the behavior you want if you were precise enough about what you rewarded.

cards.title: Supervised Learning at Depth — Labeled Data and the Train/Test Split
cards.teaser: The train/test split is one of the most important engineering decisions in an ML project, and leaking information across the boundary silently invalidates every reported metric.

runs.text: The purpose of the train/test split is to produce an unbiased estimate of how a model will behave on data it has never seen — which is always the data that matters, since training data is the past and deployment is the future.
The mechanics that go wrong in practice:

items.text: Temporal leakage

— if your data has a time dimension (transactions, user events, log entries), splitting randomly mixes future data into the training set. A model trained on random samples from a time series has seen the future and appears to perform much better than it will in deployment. The correct split is always chronological: train on data before a cutoff date, evaluate on data after it.
Entity leakage

— if the same real-world entity (customer, patient, device) can appear in both train and test, the model may memorize the entity's historical pattern and appear to generalize when it is actually overfitting to entity-level noise.
Feature leakage

— including features in training that encode information about the label, but that would not be available at prediction time. Classic example: including a fraud flag in training a fraud model because the flag was set after human review of the same transactions.
Test set reuse

— using the test set to make design decisions (pick a model architecture, tune a threshold) means the test set is no longer a fresh evaluation. Reported test set performance becomes an optimistic estimate.

runs.text: The validation set exists to absorb the decisions that would otherwise contaminate the test set. Use it freely. Touch the test set once.

Ask your team to show you the split logic, not just the split ratio. A 70/30 split that is chronologically ordered is far more informative than an 80/20 split that is randomly shuffled from a time-series dataset.
The train/test split is like a closed-book exam — if any of the exam questions leaked into the study materials, the score tells you nothing useful about what the student actually knows.

cards.title: Unsupervised Learning at Depth — Clustering and Dimensionality Reduction
cards.teaser: Clustering finds groups; dimensionality reduction finds compressed representations — and neither technique tells you whether those groups or representations are meaningful without domain judgment.

runs.text: Clustering in depth:

The key decision in clustering is not the algorithm — it is the distance metric and the number of clusters (or the density threshold). K-means requires you to specify k before you run it. DBSCAN requires you to specify a neighborhood radius and a minimum density. Both produce a different answer for every set of hyperparameters, and there is no objective test set accuracy to tell you which answer is correct.
Cluster evaluation metrics:

items.text: Silhouette score

— measures how similar each point is to its own cluster compared to neighboring clusters. A high score means tight, well-separated clusters, but says nothing about whether those clusters are semantically meaningful.
Davies-Bouldin index
— lower is better; penalizes clusters that are close together and internally dispersed.
Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares)
— useful for choosing k with an elbow plot, but always decreases as k increases, so it cannot be used alone.

runs.text: Dimensionality reduction in depth:

PCA finds linear projections that preserve maximum variance. t-SNE and UMAP find non-linear projections optimized for local structure — they are excellent for visualization but should not be used to build features for downstream models because the coordinate axes are not interpretable or stable across runs.
The critical test question for any unsupervised output: "Who has looked at a sample of these clusters and confirmed they make sense?"

As of 2026-Q2, UMAP is the dominant tool for high-dimensional visualization of embedding spaces, largely displacing t-SNE in production ML pipelines. Both are visualization tools, not feature engineering tools.

Dimensionality reduction is like making a shadow of a complex 3D sculpture — the shadow is real and structurally derived from the object, but it loses information and looks different depending on the angle of the light.

cards.title: Deep Learning vs Traditional ML
cards.teaser: Deep learning learns its own features; traditional ML requires you to engineer them — and for testers, the distinction changes where defects hide and how you find them.

runs.text: Traditional ML algorithms — logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting, support vector machines — operate on features you define and extract. A feature might be "number of transactions in the last 30 days" or "ratio of uppercase letters in an email subject line." You, the developer, decided those features were relevant. They are interpretable and auditable.

Deep learning (neural networks with multiple layers) learns its own internal representations from raw input data. You feed it pixels, text tokens, or audio waveforms, and the network learns, layer by layer, what patterns to extract. The features are latent — they exist as activations in the network's hidden layers and are generally not interpretable by a human.
What this means for your test approach:

items.text: Feature bugs in traditional ML
are findable: you can inspect the feature engineering code, check for miscalculations, and trace a wrong prediction back to a corrupted feature value.
Feature bugs in deep learning

are largely opaque: if the model learned a spurious feature (e.g., it learned to classify X-rays by the hospital watermark, not the pathology), there is no explicit feature column to audit. You find these bugs through behavioral testing — probing for systematic failures on subgroups or input perturbations.
Traditional ML
tends to be more sample-efficient and interpretable. Deep learning tends to achieve higher ceiling performance on unstructured data (images, text, audio) but requires far more data and compute.

runs.text: As of 2026-Q2, gradient boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) remains the dominant approach for tabular data in industry. Deep learning dominates on unstructured data. Many production systems combine both.

Traditional ML is like a recipe where every ingredient is listed; deep learning is like asking a master chef to cook from scratch — the result may be better, but if something tastes wrong you cannot just check the ingredient list.

cards.title: Generative AI vs Predictive AI
cards.teaser: Predictive models output a label or score; generative models output new content — and they fail in fundamentally different ways that require different test strategies.

runs.text: Predictive AI

takes an input and produces a structured output: a class label, a probability score, a regression value, a ranking. The output space is finite and defined in advance. A fraud detection model outputs a probability between 0 and 1. A spam classifier outputs "spam" or "not spam." You can measure correctness by comparing the output to a known-correct label.
Generative AI

takes an input (a prompt, an image, a seed) and produces new content: text, images, audio, code. The output space is effectively infinite, and there is rarely a single correct answer. A model asked to summarize a document could produce thousands of valid summaries, all of which are correct, and thousands more that are subtly wrong in ways that range from harmless to dangerous.
The failure modes diverge:

items.text: Predictive failures
are typically measurable — false positive rate, false negative rate, calibration error, threshold sensitivity. You can run a held-out test set and get a number.
Generative failures

include hallucination (confidently wrong factual claims), style drift, refusal (the model declines a valid request), toxicity, prompt injection (adversarial inputs that override intended behavior), and subtle semantic errors that are grammatically fluent. These failures require qualitative evaluation, red-teaming, and domain expert review — not just metric computation.

runs.text: Testing a generative system and testing a predictive system share some foundations (data quality, distributional coverage, edge cases) but diverge significantly in evaluation methodology.

As of 2026-Q2, there is no fully automated, reliable way to detect hallucination in large language model outputs at scale. Treat any automated hallucination score as a heuristic signal, not a ground truth.

Testing a predictive model is like grading a multiple-choice exam; testing a generative model is like grading an essay — the grading scale exists, but applying it requires judgment that a simple answer key cannot supply.

cards.title: The "Data is the Program" Framing
cards.teaser: In classical software the code is the system; in ML the training data is the system — and that inversion is why bugs in ML systems are so much harder to localize.

runs.text: In a classical software system, the program behavior is fully determined by the source code. If the system does something wrong, the root cause is in the code — a wrong condition, an off-by-one error, a missing null check. You can, in principle, read the code and reason about every possible output.

In an ML system, the source code defines the model architecture and the training procedure. But the model's behavior on any specific input is determined by the billions of parameter values that were produced by training on data. Those parameter values are not written by a developer. They are derived, through an optimization process, from the training dataset.
The implications for testing:

items.text: You cannot read the model.
You can inspect the weights of a neural network, but you cannot reason from those weights to expected behavior on an input. The model is opaque by construction.
Bugs can come from data, not code.
A model that systematically underperforms on a demographic group may be exhibiting a perfect implementation of a biased training process. The code is correct; the data was the defect.
Fixing a data bug requires re-training.
You cannot patch the behavior the way you patch code. You must correct the data, retrain the model, and re-evaluate — a much longer feedback loop than a code fix.
The same code, different data, produces a different system.
Two runs of the same training pipeline on different datasets produce different models with different behaviors. The code version alone does not identify the system.

runs.text: When logging the version of an ML system for reproducibility or incident investigation, record the dataset version, the training run ID, and the evaluation metrics — not just the code commit hash.

The training data in an ML system is like the DNA in a living organism — the code is the developmental process, but what the organism actually does in the world is determined by the genetic material, not just the instructions for how to grow.

cards.title: Probabilistic Outputs — Confidence Scores and Calibration
cards.teaser: Most ML models output a probability, not a decision — and whether that probability means what you think it means depends on calibration, which most teams never check.

runs.text: A classifier trained with a softmax output layer produces a probability distribution over classes. The model might say: "This email is spam with probability 0.91." That number looks like a confidence, but it is only a confidence if the model is calibrated.
Calibration

means: among all inputs where the model says 0.9, approximately 90% should actually belong to the positive class. A model is well-calibrated if its stated probabilities match empirical frequencies. Many models are not well-calibrated out of the box — they are overconfident (saying 0.95 when the empirical rate is 0.70) or underconfident (saying 0.60 when the empirical rate is 0.80).
Why calibration matters for testing:

items.text: Thresholds are set against these scores.
If you set a decision threshold at 0.7 and the model's 0.7 corresponds to a 0.50 empirical frequency, your operational threshold is effectively random.
Confidence scores drive downstream systems.
A model that feeds its confidence into a risk scoring system or a human review queue must be calibrated for that downstream system to behave as designed.
Post-hoc calibration is possible.
Platt scaling and isotonic regression are two common techniques for recalibrating a model's output probabilities after training. Both require a held-out calibration set.

runs.text: Calibration evaluation tool: reliability diagrams (also called calibration curves) plot predicted probability on the x-axis against observed frequency on the y-axis. A perfectly calibrated model falls on the diagonal.

A model that achieves high AUC-ROC can still be poorly calibrated. These are different properties of the model's output. High AUC means the model ranks positives above negatives; calibration means the absolute probability values are accurate. Both matter for downstream decisions.

Calibration is the difference between a weather forecaster who says "70% chance of rain" and means it — and one who says "70%" whenever they lean toward rain, regardless of how strong that lean actually is.

cards.title: The ML Lifecycle — A Tester's Map
cards.teaser: ML systems are not delivered once and maintained — they are continuously re-trained, re-evaluated, and re-deployed, and every transition in that lifecycle is a potential point of failure.

runs.text: The ML lifecycle is the end-to-end process from problem definition to production monitoring. Understanding its shape tells you where your testing activities belong and what risks are live at each stage.
The stages:

items.text: Data collection and preparation

— acquiring raw data, labeling it (for supervised learning), cleaning it, and splitting it into train/validation/test sets. Defects here propagate through every subsequent stage. Testing activities: data quality checks, label audits, distribution analysis, leakage detection.
Model training

— running the optimization process to fit model parameters to the training data. Defects here include incorrect loss functions, wrong hyperparameters, and training instability. Testing activities: loss curve inspection, sanity checks (does a model trained on random labels perform at chance?), reproducibility checks.
Model evaluation

— measuring performance on the held-out test set and on targeted subgroups. This is the stage most teams treat as "the testing phase," but it is only one of many. Testing activities: metric computation, fairness analysis, calibration checks, behavioral testing.
Deployment

— packaging the model and making it available to consume requests. Defects here include pipeline mismatches (the feature engineering in serving differs from training), latency regressions, and rollout issues. Testing activities: shadow mode testing, A/B testing, canary deployments.
Monitoring

— observing the deployed model's behavior over time, detecting data drift (the distribution of incoming requests shifts away from the training distribution) and concept drift (the relationship between inputs and correct outputs changes). Testing activities: statistical process control on prediction distributions, monitoring model metrics against golden datasets, alert thresholds on input feature distributions.

runs.text: Chapter 3 covers evaluation in depth. Chapter 8 covers monitoring and drift detection.

Most ML defects are found in production, not during offline evaluation — because the real-world input distribution is almost never fully captured in the training and test sets. Monitoring is not optional; it is where testing continues after deployment.

The ML lifecycle is like a manufacturing line where the raw material is data, the factory is your training infrastructure, and the quality inspector is you — except the product changes its own behavior over time as the world it operates in drifts, so the inspection never ends.
